description: F1 introduced a $145M budget cap for 2021, aiming to level the playing field between top teams (Mercedes, Red Bull, Ferrari) and midfield/backmarker teams. Support grew from ~55% to ~72% as closer racing materialized, though top-team fans remained skeptical.

key_events.description: Budget cap officially enacted at $145M for 2021 season. Smaller teams (Williams, Haas, Alfa Romeo) strongly support. Mercedes and Red Bull express concerns about job cuts.
2021 season delivers Hamilton vs. Verstappen title fight — closest championship battle in years. Fans credit competitive balance partly to cap anticipation and rule changes.
Red Bull accused of exceeding the 2021 cap by $1.8M. FIA investigation begins. Debate over enforcement credibility.
FIA confirms Red Bull minor overspend breach. $7M fine and 10% wind tunnel reduction penalty. Mixed reaction — some say too lenient, others accept it.
2023 season shows midfield convergence. McLaren and Aston Martin become competitive. Cap widely credited with improving the sport's competitive balance.

calibration_notes: Steady upward trajectory in support driven by visible competitive improvements. Opposition concentrated among top-team fans and purists who valued engineering arms race. Red Bull cost cap breach briefly dented credibility but enforcement response stabilized opinion. Tests: institutional reform acceptance, gradual evidence-based persuasion, enforcement credibility effects.
